gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Follow-up for perioral dermatitis.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Jacqueline Miller is a 40-year-old female who is being seen today for a follow-up skin exam for perioral dermatitis. She also has a history of rosacea, including ocular rosacea. Today, she states that her perioral dermatitis is well-controlled, although it has not fully resolved. The patient was applying doxycycline twice a day with improvement, but her pharmacist advised her to reduce to once daily since it had started to improve; therefore, for approximately the past week, she has been using the doxycycline only once per day. She has not seen continued improvement but it has not worsened. She is using the sulfacetamide wash twice daily, denies drying of the skin, and reports that her skin is much improved moisturization. She also applies MetroCream immediately after using the sulfacetamide wash. She denies using other lotions and products, Cetaphil lotion or soap, or Neutrogena wipes. The patient did not begin Elidel because it is not covered by her insurance, and she was concerned about possible side effects from the medication.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Perioral dermatitis.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Doxycycline cream. Apply twice daily.
MetroCream. 
Sulfacetamide wash. Apply topically twice daily.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Integumentary
Skin inspection of the face was performed today. On bilateral medial cheeks, there are a few, very faint, erythematous papules with mild redness around and underneath the nostrils. Fitzpatrick skin type is 4 to 5.

ASSESSMENT

 Perioral dermatitis
 Rosacea, including ocular rosacea

Jacqueline Miller is a 40-year-old female who presented today for follow-up for her perioral dermatitis, which has improved but not resolved. She decreased her doxycycline per her pharmacist's recommendation, which possibly stagnated the resolution of symptoms.

PLAN

Perioral dermatitis.
I counseled the patient about the risks and benefits of doxycycline and recommended she resume twice daily application until she reaches full resolution, and at that point she should reduce application to once a day for an additional week. The patient will not be using Elidel as I reviewed the cost of the medication and without insurance, it was too expensive. I provided a prescription for Tacrolimus and counseled her on possible side effects of using the medication. She will continue with Sulfacetamide face wash once a day. Also, use Bruder mask as needed.

Rosacea, including ocular rosacea.
Continue using MetroCream twice daily. I recommended to gently wash her eyelids once daily with a gentle cleanser and try a gentle cream such as Vanicream.

INSTRUCTIONS

Call the clinic if symptoms worsen. The patient will have a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Jacqueline Miller, presents with a rash on her face, which she describes as a small bit left over. She reports that she had been taking doxycycline twice a day for the first two weeks, but then reduced the dose to once a day, which she believes has resulted in a plateau in her improvement. She denies any fever. The patient is not breast-feeding. Past medical history is not mentioned. Review of systems is not mentioned. Current medications include doxycycline, sulfacetamide wash, and metrocream.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's Fitzpatrick skin type is IV, indicating she will not burn and will suntan easily. There are a few faint erythematous papules and redness on her bilateral medial cheeks and around her nostrils.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has perioral dermatitis, rosacea, including ocular rosacea.
Problem list: Perioral dermatitis; rosacea; ocular rosacea; unknown status on all three.

Plan:
I prescribed doxycycline 100mg twice a day for the rash. I instructed the patient to continue using the sulfacetamide wash and metrocream. I recommended that she apply tacrolimus ointment to the rash area twice a day until it is resolved, and then once a day for a week before stopping. I instructed her to continue doxycycline for another week after the rash is gone, and then stop it. I told her to continue using the wash and metrocream after stopping doxycycline. I instructed her to hold off on using her old products until her next follow-up appointment. I provided her with a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks. I recommended that she use a basic moisturizer like vanicream or petroleum jelly, and provided her with samples. I instructed her to stay away from anti-aging products and plant-based products.

Follow-up:
The patient is scheduled for a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks.

HPI: Jacqueline Miller presented with a history of perioral dermatitis, rosacea, and ocular rosacea. She reports that her condition has been improving with doxycycline, but has plateaued since switching from twice a day to once a day dosing. She also mentions a small residual rash on her face.

Past medical history: Not mentioned.

Review of systems: Not mentioned.

Current medications: 
- Doxycycline 1 tablet once a day for acne.

Problem list: Perioral dermatitis; rosacea; ocular rosacea; status: active.

Plan:
I instructed the patient to continue using the sulfacetamide wash once a day, the metrocream after washing her face, and to increase the doxycycline to twice a day. I also recommended the patient pick up the tacrolimus ointment and start using it on the rash area twice a day until the rash resolves. After the rash is gone, she will stop the tacrolimus ointment and continue the doxycycline for another week before stopping. She will also continue to use the sulfacetamide wash and metrocream. I will have her return in 4 weeks for a follow-up.

Follow-up: I scheduled a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks to reassess the patient's condition.

She has been using sulfacetamide wash twice a day and metrocream after washing her face, and has not used any other lotions or cleansers. Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Jacqueline Miller, presented with a rash on her face, which she described as a small bit left over from a previous issue. She started using doxycycline once a day, but noticed it plateaued and is now experiencing a few faint erythematous papules on bilateral medial cheeks and some redness around and underneath her nostrils. She stopped breast-feeding and started using doxycycline once a day after reading about the oral form of Elidel, which she was worried about due to potential cancer risks associated with long-term use. She is using sulfacetamide wash twice a day, metrocream, and is not using any lotions or other products. Past medical history: not mentioned. Review of systems: not mentioned. Current medications: doxycycline (twice a day, for rash), sulfacetamide wash (twice a day), metrocream (after face wash).

Objective:
Vital signs: not mentioned. Physical exam: Fitzpatrick skin type IV, bilateral medial cheeks with a few faint erythematous papules, and redness around and underneath the nostrils. Test results: not mentioned.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has perioral dermatitis, rosacea including ocular rosacea. Problem list: Perioral dermatitis; rosacea; ocular rosacea; confirmed.

Plan:
I recommended that the patient continue using the same face wash, metrocream, and increase the use of doxycycline to twice a day. I instructed her to apply the tacrolimus ointment to the rash on her face twice daily until it resolves, then once a day for another week before stopping. I also instructed her to continue doxycycline for another week after the rash resolves, and then stop. I advised her to use the wash and metrocream long-term. I will see her for a follow-up in 4 weeks and instructed her to reach out to me if she has any questions or if she starts to flare up again.

Follow-up:
I scheduled a follow-up appointment for 4 weeks to check on the patient's progress. I also instructed her to reach out to me if she has any questions or if she starts to flare up again.
